props.covers: The FigJam-parity chrome layer: standalone, unwired UI components (dock, selection toolbar, color palette, shape surfaces, zoom controls) built pixel-first against reference recordings, before being wired into the live editor.

text.insert: and implements the FigJam-parity surface UI for the canvas engine: the bottom tool dock, the selection-aware floating toolbar (hosted from
), the one color picker, the two shape-browsing surfaces, and the zoom pill. It was built per the spec docs under

(figjam-bottom-dock-spec.md, figjam-chrome-catalog.md, figjam-style-tokens.json/figjam-style-spec.md) and is documented in the components directory's own README.md, which this doc summarizes and cross-links.

props.title: Build chrome standalone and unwired, then wire it in a later pass

text.insert: was built with zero imports from the live editor — no

— and zero edits to those files. This was deliberate: it let the chrome surface be developed, styled, and pixel-matched against FigJam reference recordings entirely on its own, in parallel with unrelated model and interaction work, and wired into the live editor only in a later pass (W3-wire) once the visual layer was settled. Two concrete benefits follow from the isolation: the UI work is parallelizable against other tracks touching the editor internals, and each component's test can assert measured geometry (stadium radius, colors, button counts, group/divider structure) against plain props in jsdom, with no dependency on canvas interaction state, model wiring, or a running editor instance.
— the shared dark hover-label used by every other component below.
— 13 icon components plus 2 zoom glyphs: inline SVG glyphs for the bottom dock and the zoom pill.
— the white stadium bottom dock (renamed from FigJamDock; the FIGJAM_DOCK_*_PX size exports remain): content-fit x 53px, 6 buttons across 2 groups with 1 vertical divider.
— toolbar glyphs now live in the shared icon set and are resolved per action id by
table, so control specs on object defs stay icon-free.
: a pure clamp-to-viewport positioning function fed the measured toolbar size (a ResizeObserver in the hook; height defaults to EDITOR_STYLE.selectionToolbarHeightPx = 48 before first measure).
— the dark floating selection toolbar: a generic host rendering a
swatch row, identical for every kind, with a purple rounded-square outline on the current pick. Replaces the old ColorPalettePopover, the section-tint flyout, and the connector swatch row.
— shape catalog ARRANGEMENT (categories, ordering, direction/icon variants), deliberately data-only: entry labels/keywords derive from each def's
metadata (P4), and the JSX preview glyphs live editor-side in shape-previews.tsx.

(icon glyphs excluded, since swap changes an existing object's type).
— the full-height left-docked white Shapes sidebar (Panel B), invoked from the bottom dock's shape-tool button.

— this is the dock spec's "modal relinquishing" rule (fj-053 onward): no dock button shows active/violet while the Shapes panel or another surface owns focus. The consuming editor must derive this prop from the current interaction mode, not from "last tool used"; connector is one of the reducer tools the dock reflects. The interactive mockup the redesign was approved against is

props.title: "multi" selection toolbar is computed, not reference-verified

text.insert: The multi-select toolbar is no longer a hand-authored variant: it is computed as the capability intersection of the selected defs' control lists via

. The caveat stands, though: the source FigJam reference recording never captured a multi-select toolbar, so the intersection's ordering and control choice were never verified against the real product. Anyone relying on exact multi-select control sets should check live FigJam first.

props.title: Toolbar control lists are data-only on ObjectDefs; the chrome host is generic

text.insert: Each object def carries an icon-free
); a multi-select shows the capability intersection via
table and per-action state through the
map, while flyout components live in an editor-side registry keyed by def kind + action id:

. Object defs never import React chrome; the live toolbar layer adds the editor-side per-kind state it must know about — section border/fit state, object color previews, and selected-connection color/control state — before passing generic controls to the host.

props.title: Toolbar controls read left-to-right by expected usage frequency

text.insert: Toolbar controls read left-to-right by expected usage frequency. Every object's toolbar starts with the universal Color + Text pair, the most-used actions, always adjacent and never separated; type-specific modifiers come next; state controls such as Lock sit last. Concrete orders are: Section: Color, Rename | Fit to content, Border style | Lock; Shape: Fill color, Edit text | Change shape; Sticky: Color, Edit text; Connector: Line color, Text | Line style, Arrowheads. Separators divide usage groups, not individual items:
marks the last control of a group in

props.title: Connector toolbar exposes line color, label text, dash, and arrowheads

text.insert: The connector toolbar is exactly Line color, Text, Line style, Arrowhead style from
; the earlier corner-style/routing control is gone. The dash and arrowhead flyouts are compact icon rows: solid/dashed pictures for
. Collapsed toolbar buttons are value-reflective via

: they mirror the selected connector's current style and arrowhead, treating absent fields as solid/forward and falling back to solid/forward when a mixed connection set disagrees. The Text control opens routed-midpoint connection label editing with

props.title: Dock order and hotkeys map positionally to the left-hand home row

text.insert: Ford uses a split-hand keyboard, so the bottom dock is designed for one-handed left-side tool switching: reading the dock left-to-right equals reading the keys left-to-right, in the same order you would click them. Dock order is Sticky, Section, Shapes | Select, Hand, Connector (two groups of three, one divider). Keys are
shapes (toggles the Shapes panel, same code path as the dock button),
connector — the modifier chord sits on the far-left button, then the plain home row
shape-placement keys remain. Hotkeys route through the same dock-selection handler as clicks (
arms the dedicated Connector Mode tool, and the button lights violet-active like any other armed tool. Constraint:
is taken by sticky, so a future select-all-objects binding requires rehoming sticky. Any new dock tool must slot into this positional scheme — its key is its dock position, not a mnemonic.

props.title: FigJam-scale chrome, deliberately larger than the measured baseline

text.insert: The live toolbar supersedes the measured 29px pill from the reference recordings: it is a 48px rounded-rect bar (radius 14) with 36px buttons and a 28px color swatch, all sourced from
). All flyouts open ABOVE the toolbar, centered on their trigger, on the shared
). The pixel-matched 29px measurements remain documented history, not the shipping geometry.

props.title: One color pick per object, one picker for every kind

text.insert: Every kind — shape, sticky, section, connector — stores one
pick and offers the same 10 hue picks through the same

role) decides how the pick renders via palette.ts's role tables. A section pick sets tint + title-chip colors together (the section border IS the chip fill); there are no separate fill/stroke palettes and no section-specific tint row anymore. The section border-style flyout is icon-only using single-line solid/dashed/none glyphs (
is both schema-valid and offered. Section lock remains its own flyout — Lock all / Lock background only / Unlock — writing the schema's

props.title: Black pop-up motion and elevation follow the FigJam-measured recipe

text.insert: The selection toolbar enters with opacity 0→1 plus a 6px upward slide over 140ms on
— the exact curve measured frame-by-frame from FigJam recordings (reference doc with live demos and measured tables:
override. The animation replays on every appearance and selection-identity change —

(variant + connection id + selected ids, never viewport) keys the pill so React remounts it; pan/zoom repositioning never replays. Exits are an instant cut and flyouts never animate — FigJam's "animate in, cut out" asymmetry. Elevation is three shadow layers on
: a 0.5px zero-blur ring (the hairline "border" on the measured bars), a tight key shadow, and a wide ambient halo (

props.title: The toolbar hides during selection drags and reappears animated on drop

text.insert: While a gesture that manipulates the selection's geometry is active —
unmounts the toolbar (closing any open flyout) so the canvas stays visible mid-drag. Dropping remounts the pill, which replays the entrance animation for free. Sub-threshold presses never hide it (
only starts past the drag threshold, so plain clicks don't flicker), and
) as a single 10-swatch row on the dark FigJam popover chrome: 30px circular swatches, 12px gaps, 14px panel padding on the radius-16 popover, and a hairline
ring on every swatch (0.3 for near-white swatches), resolving each preview hex via
— previews are identical for every kind (D12: a pick is a direction; the kind's role table decides rendering).
rounded-square outline (radius 9, about 3px outside the swatch); the rainbow conic ring is retired;

The FigJam source reference recording shows only "-"/"+" controls, with no percentage readout ever visible.

Each chrome component has a focused jsdom test under

that asserts measured geometry — stadium radius, background colors, button/control counts, group/divider DOM structure — as style and DOM assertions rather than visual screenshots. This follows from the same no-routes, standalone-component constraint that shaped the build: without a running editor or a rendered page to screenshot, tests instead pin down the concrete numbers and structure a pixel-matched component must produce (e.g. exact swatch counts, exact button groupings) so a regression shows up as a failing assertion rather than a diff someone has to eyeball. Run the suite with
from the canvas package root (selection-toolbar tests live beside the feature under src/editor/features/selection-toolbar/__tests__/).
For how the chrome layer is exercised and pixel-checked in an actual running environment — the Storybook-style studio harness and sample canvas data used to develop and review these components — see
